content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento. CA 958 I ➔-2329 
(91 o) 322-5660 • Fa., (916) 322-0886 
Patricia Cowett and Brian Hebert 
California Law Revision Commission 
c/o UC Davis School of Law 
400 Mrak Hall Drive, Room 1128 
Davis, CA 95616 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-12-106 
July 5, 2012 
Dear Commissioner Cowett and Mr. Hebert: 
This letter responds to Mr. Hebert's request for further advice, on behalf of 
Commissioner Cowett. regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act1 
in connection with our previous letter to Commissioner Cowett. (1-12-054 ). Mr. Hebert has 
provided additional information regarding the matters raised in Commissioner Cowett's request 
for advice. Because we lacked sufficient information at that time to enable us to provide formal 
advice, we provided informal assistance. We now address the same question raised in the prior 
request based on the additional information. 
The question raised by Commissioner Cowett is whether her condominium, considered 
under applicable law to be a "unit" in a '"common interest development," gives rise to a 
disqualifying conflict of interest in decisions of the California Law Revision Commission (the 
"Revision Commission") that involve common interest developments. The additional 
information suggests that the rule referred to colloquially as the "public generally exception" 
applies to Commissioner Cowett's facts. Our advice in this letter will therefore be limited to 
discussion of the "public generally" portion of the Act's eight-step conflict of interest analysis. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 l000 through 91 O 14. All statutory 
rderences are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Tille 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-12-106 
Page No. 2 
The facts are substantially the same as those set forth in the Cowett Advice Letter, No. 
I- 12-054, which are incorporated by reference. The California Revision Commission studies 
problems in statutory law, as authorized by the Legislature, in order to recommend reforms. The 
Revision Commission does not have power to enact legislation; rather, it makes 
recommendations to the Legislature. 
Mr. Hebert has identified the following decisions that have previously come before the 
Revision Commission involving common interest developments as indicative of decisions that 
will come before the Revision Commission in the future: 
• Improvement of the informal dispute resolution processes; 
• Creation of "notice and comment" rule making procedures; 
• Creation of basic procedural rules for architectural review and decisionmaking; 
• Technical improvements to the organization and expression of common interest 
development law; 
• Minor improvements to the rules governing association operations; 
• Creation of a state ombudsman for common interest development matters; 
Mr. Hebert states that future decisions will likely address technical problems in the 
existing statutes and recommendations for minor improvements to governance processes and 
owner rights. The reforms contemplated by these decisions are likely to make common interest 
development governance more efficient, with less need to consult attorneys and litigate, and 
avoid problems that might otherwise develop from poor self-governance or disagreements about 
the extent of owner rights. 
THE PuBLIC GENERALL V EXCEPTION 
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, "distinguishable 
from its effect on the public generally," on the official, the official's real property, or other 
enumerated individuals or entities. Regulation 18707 sets forth the requirements for the public 
generally exception to apply. 
In order to take advantage of the public generally rule, Ms. Cowett must meet a two-part 
test by showing that the decision would affect a "significant segment" of the public in 
"substantially the same manner" as it financially affects her economic interest. 
' 
For decisions that affect a public official's real property, a .. significant segment'' is 
defined as: 
"(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all residential property owners 
in the jurisdiction of the official· s agency or the district the official represents; or 


File No. A-12-106 
Page No. 3 
"(ii) 5,000 property owners or residential property owners in the jurisdiction of 
the official's agency." (Regulation 18707.l(b)(l)(B).) 
We have not been provided information regarding the number of property owners or 
residential property owners who own units in common interest developments in California - the 
jurisdiction of the Revision Commission. However, Commissioner Cowett states that there are 
over 40,000 common interest developments in California comprised of 4,000,000 separately 
owned units. Even if we were to assume that there are many property owners who own more 
than one unit and that the units may be located in more than one common interest development, 
it is readily apparent that there are at least 5,000 property owners in the state that own a unit in a 
common interest development. Therefore, the "significant segment" threshold is met. 
The second prong of the analysis requires that Commissioner Cowett's economic interest 
be affected in "substantially the same manner" as the "significant segment." If the answer is 
"yes" as to the economic interest identified, then the effect of the decision is not distinguishable 
from the effect on the public generally and Commissioner Cowett may participate in the 
decision. (Regulation 18707(b)(4).) To assess whether a significant segment will be affected in 
substantially the same manner, all measurable effects from the decision must be identified and 
compared. 
If Commissioner Cowett owns one unit in a single common interest development and the 
changes to the laws that are the subject of the Revision Commission's decisions apply to all 
common interest developments in the same way, this prong of the test would also be met. So 
long as this is the case, Commissioner Cowett's condominium will not present a disqualifying 
conflict of interest, and she may participate in the above-described decisions. Please note, 
however, that a conflict of interest arises only from a specific decision that can affect an 
official's economic interests. Therefore, Commissioner Cowett will need to analyze each 
decision on a case-by-case basis, using the guidelines set forth in this and our previous letter, to 
determine whether she has a disqualifying conflict of interest with respect to that decision. 2 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
'· 
By: 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
General Counsel 
 
Valentina Joyce 
Counsel, Legal Division 
9 For example, you noted that the Commissioner may also consider .. separate (and less extensive) 
regulation of nonresidential common interest developments:· Depending on the nature of these future decisions and 
surrounding circumstances. lhe .. public generally .. exception may not apply. Thus. we are unable to advise herein 
on this question.

sections.question: Whether her condominium, considered under applicable law to be a "unit" in a "common interest development," gives rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest in decisions of the California Law Revision Commission (the "Revision Commission") that involve common interest developments.
sections.conclusion: So long as changes to the laws that are the subject of the Revision Commission's decisions apply to all common interest developments in the same way, Commissioner Cowett's condominium will not present a disqualifying conflict of interest, and she may participate in the described decisions. However, Commissioner Cowett will need to analyze each decision on a case-by-case basis to determine whether she has a disqualifying conflict of interest with respect to that decision.

sections.analysis: of the "public generally" portion of the Act's eight-step conflict of interest analysis.
sections.question_synthetic: Does Commissioner Cowett have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act regarding her condominium ownership when the Revision Commission considers matters affecting common interest developments?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that the "public generally exception" applies to Commissioner Cowett's situation, allowing her to participate in Revision Commission decisions affecting common interest developments generally, provided the legal changes apply uniformly across all such developments and affect her economic interests in substantially the same manner as a significant segment of property owners.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, old; LLM: Follow-up to prior informal advice letter A-12-054; provides formal advice based on additional information; clearly structured with FACTS and THE PUBLIC GENERALLY EXCEPTION sections; includes detailed regulatory analysis of the two-part test for the public generally exception.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether her condominium, considered under applicable law to be a "unit" in a "common interest development," gives rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest in decisions of the California Law Revision Commission (the "Revision Commission") that involve common interest developments.

CONCLUSION: So long as changes to the laws that are the subject of the Revision Commission's decisions apply to all common interest developments in the same way, Commissioner Cowett's condominium will not present a disqualifying conflict of interest, and she may participate in the described decisions. However, Commissioner Cowett will need to analyze each decision on a case-by-case basis to determine whether she has a disqualifying conflict of interest with respect to that decision.

embedding.summary: This advice letter from the FPPC addresses whether Commissioner Patricia Cowett has a disqualifying conflict of interest regarding her condominium ownership when participating in California Law Revision Commission decisions involving common interest developments. The FPPC concludes that the "public generally exception" applies, permitting her participation in such decisions on a case-by-case basis.
